county_metadata.record_losses: 1866 fire and Civil War losses damaged some early courthouse records; partial survival
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Van Buren County formed 1833 from Conway, Independence, and Izard counties. County seat is Clinton. The 1866 fire and Civil War losses destroyed early courthouse records. Greers Ferry Lake (1963) inundated some communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Van Buren County suffered an 1866 fire and Civil War losses that destroyed early courthouse records. The County Clerk's office in Clinton holds surviving records. This Ozark county was remote and sparsely populated, with subsistence farming as the primary economy. Greers Ferry Lake (built 1963) inundated some communities and their records. The Van Buren County Historical Society maintains reconstructed records. For pre-fire families, check parent county records (Conway, Independence, Izard) and GLO land patents. Small church and cemetery records are critical sources. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Van Buren County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Clinton holds deed books from 1833

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Washington County formed 1828 from Crawford County. County seat is Fayetteville. Home to the University of Arkansas. Site of the Battle of Prairie Grove (1862). Now one of Arkansas's most populous counties in the northwest Arkansas growth corridor.
county_metadata.research_tips: Washington County records at the County Clerk's office in Fayetteville are well-preserved from 1828. The University of Arkansas in Fayetteville houses the Special Collections department with extensive Arkansas and Ozark regional manuscripts, photographs, and oral histories. The Shiloh Museum of Ozark History in Springdale is an outstanding regional resource. The Battle of Prairie Grove (1862), fought in Washington County, was a major Civil War engagement -- check military records. The Washington County Historical Society maintains research files. Northwest Arkansas has grown dramatically, but historical records reflect its Ozark frontier origins. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Washington County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Fayetteville holds deed books from 1828

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: White County formed 1835 from Independence, Jackson, and Pulaski counties. County seat is Searcy (not to be confused with Searcy County). Home to Harding University. Located in the transition zone between the Ozark foothills and the delta.
county_metadata.research_tips: White County records at the County Clerk's office in Searcy are complete from 1835. Searcy (the county seat -- not to be confused with Searcy County) is home to Harding University, whose Brackett Library has regional history collections. The White County Historical Society in Searcy maintains research files. This county spans the transition zone between the Ozark foothills and the delta lowlands. Kensett, Judsonia, and Beebe are other significant communities in the county. The White County Genealogical Society has published cemetery surveys and family records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for White County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Searcy holds deed books from 1835

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Woodruff County formed 1862 from Jackson and St. Francis counties during the Civil War. County seat is Augusta, on the White River. Located in the delta cotton and timber region.
county_metadata.research_tips: Woodruff County records at the County Clerk's office in Augusta are complete from 1862. Formed during the Civil War, this White River county was a cotton and timber area. Augusta, on the White River, was a steamboat town. The Woodruff County Historical Society maintains local research files. The county has a significant African American population, and Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are important. Cotton plantation records in estate inventories are detailed. The Augusta Public Library has local history materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Check Jackson and St. Francis county records for earlier families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Woodruff County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Augusta holds deed books from 1862

county_metadata.record_losses: 1864 Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; records severely damaged
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Yell County formed 1840 from Pope and Scott counties. County seat is Danville (also Dardanelle for the southern district). Named for Archibald Yell, first congressman from Arkansas. The 1864 Civil War courthouse burning severely damaged records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yell County's courthouse was burned during the Civil War in 1864, severely damaging records. The County Clerk's office in Danville holds surviving and post-war records. The county has a dual courthouse system with Dardanelle. Dardanelle, on the Arkansas River, was a significant steamboat landing. The county was named for Archibald Yell, first congressman from Arkansas and governor. Lake Dardanelle provides water resources for the area. The Yell County Historical and Genealogical Society maintains reconstructed records. For pre-war families, check parent county records (Pope, Scott) and GLO land patents. FamilySearch has microfilmed surviving county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Federal land patents (GLO) for Yell County available at BLM website; County Clerk's office in Danville holds deed books from 1850

source_profiles.survival_notes: Early deed books damaged or destroyed; 1864 Civil War destruction; courthouse burned; records severely damaged. Post-loss records at County Clerk's office are generally complete
